questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with acute central chest pain radiating to the left arm. Symptoms began after her daughter's wedding. ECG shows ST elevation in leads V2–V5. High-sensitivity troponin I is 450 ng/L (normal <34). Coronary angiography reveals no obstructive coronary disease. Left ventriculography shows apical ballooning with hypercontractile basal segments. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The classic presentation of Takotsubo cardiomyopathy is a post-menopausal woman with emotional stress, chest pain, ST elevation, elevated troponin, and apical ballooning with normal coronaries. STEMI is the main mimic but ruled out by normal angiogram.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man is admitted with acute dyspnoea after lifting a heavy object. He has no cardiac history. ECG shows deep symmetrical T-wave inversion in leads I, aVL, V4–V6. High-sensitivity troponin I is 200 ng/L (normal <34). Transthoracic echocardiogram reveals mid-ventricular hypokinesis with preserved apical and basal contractility. Coronary angiography shows no obstructive disease. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Hypertensive crisis with acute heart failure
questions.explanation: Mid-ventricular Takotsubo is an atypical variant often triggered by physical stress, especially in men. The pattern of wall motion abnormality (mid-ventricular hypokinesis with apical and basal sparing) differentiates it from typical apical Takotsubo and other diagnoses.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman who presented with chest pain and was diagnosed with Takotsubo cardiomyopathy two days ago becomes acutely hypotensive (BP 85/50 mmHg) and develops a new systolic murmur at the left sternal edge. ECG shows sinus tachycardia with persistent T-wave inversion. Urgent echocardiogram reveals significant systolic anterior motion of the mitral valve with a left ventricular outflow tract gradient of 60 mmHg. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Left ventricular outflow tract obstruction due to hyperdynamic basal segments
Acute mitral regurgitation from chordal rupture
questions.explanation: LVOT obstruction is a well‑recognised complication of Takotsubo cardiomyopathy, caused by hypercontractile basal segments that narrow the outflow tract and produce a gradient. The murmur and hypotension are typical, whereas papillary or septal rupture are more characteristic of MI.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman is admitted to intensive care following a subarachnoid haemorrhage from a ruptured aneurysm. ECG shows deep T-wave inversions in the precordial leads. High-sensitivity troponin I is 350 ng/L (normal <34). Echocardiogram reveals apical akinesia with basal hyperkinesis. Coronary angiography shows no obstructive lesions. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Intracranial haemorrhage causing ECG changes without myocardial pathology
Pulmonary embolism with right ventricular strain
Acute coronary syndrome due to plaque rupture
questions.explanation: Neurogenic stress cardiomyopathy is a form of Takotsubo cardiomyopathy triggered by intracranial catastrophes like subarachnoid haemorrhage. The characteristic apical ballooning with normal coronaries distinguishes it from ACS. ECG changes alone without wall motion abnormality would suggest intracranial effect.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman presents with dyspnoea and pleuritic chest pain. ECG shows widespread T-wave inversion. Troponin I is 250 ng/L (normal <34). Echocardiogram shows apical hypokinesis and a 1.5 cm mobile echogenic mass at the apex. Coronary angiography is normal. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Acute myocardial infarction with mural thrombus
Left atrial myxoma embolising to the apex
questions.explanation: Apical thrombus is a known complication of Takotsubo cardiomyopathy due to regional wall motion abnormality. The normal coronaries favour Takotsubo over MI. Endocarditis is unlikely without systemic signs, and myxoma typically originates in the left atrium.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman presents with acute central chest pain after her daughter's wedding. ECG shows ST elevation in V2-V5. Troponin is 12x ULN. She has no cardiac risk factors. What is the single most appropriate investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Coronary angiography is essential to exclude obstructive coronary artery disease, the main differential. Left ventriculogram shows characteristic apical ballooning confirming Takotsubo.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman presents with dyspnoea and chest discomfort after a family argument. ECG shows deep T-wave inversions across the precordial leads. Troponin is mildly elevated at 3x ULN. Transthoracic echocardiogram shows apical akinesis with hyperdynamic basal segments. She is haemodynamically stable. What is the single most appropriate next investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Cardiac MRI can confirm the diagnosis by showing oedema on T2 imaging and absence of late gadolinium enhancement, differentiating from infarction and myocarditis. In a stable patient, this is preferred over invasive angiography.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents with chest pain after a physiotherapy session. ECG is normal. Troponin is 2x ULN. Echocardiogram shows global hypokinesis. He has no prior cardiac history. What is the single most appropriate investigation to differentiate Takotsubo cardiomyopathy from acute myocarditis?
options: Cardiac MRI with T2 mapping and LGE
questions.explanation: Cardiac MRI with T2 mapping and LGE can differentiate: Takotsubo typically shows oedema (high T2) without LGE, whereas myocarditis often shows patchy LGE. This is the key discriminator.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman with a history of anxiety presents with chest pain and breathlessness. ECG shows T-wave inversion in leads I, aVL, V5-V6. Troponin is 4x ULN. She undergoes coronary angiography which shows no obstructive disease. Left ventriculogram shows apical ballooning. Which of the following findings is required to fulfill the diagnostic criteria for Takotsubo cardiomyopathy?
options: Transient wall motion abnormality beyond a single coronary territory
questions.explanation: The Mayo Clinic criteria require transient left ventricular wall motion abnormality that does not correspond to a single epicardial coronary distribution, which is characteristic of Takotsubo. While normal coronaries are also required, the specific pattern of wall motion abnormality is the hallmark.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with chronic kidney disease (eGFR 25 ml/min) presents with chest pain and dyspnoea after a fall. ECG shows ST depression in V2-V4. Troponin is 5x ULN. What is the most appropriate investigation to establish the diagnosis of Takotsubo cardiomyopathy while minimising contrast exposure?
questions.explanation: Transthoracic echocardiogram is non-invasive, uses no contrast, and can demonstrate the typical apical ballooning pattern. It is the safest initial test to raise suspicion and is appropriate in this patient with poor renal function.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman presents with acute chest pain and dyspnoea. ECG shows deep T-wave inversions in the precordial leads, and troponin is mildly elevated. Coronary angiography reveals no obstructive coronary disease, and left ventriculography shows apical ballooning. Her left ventricular ejection fraction is 50% with no evidence of heart failure or arrhythmia. What is the most appropriate initial management?
questions.explanation: Takotsubo cardiomyopathy typically resolves spontaneously without specific pharmacotherapy. Beta-blockers, ACE inhibitors, and diuretics are not indicated first-line in the absence of heart failure or complications. Aspirin is not required as coronary arteries are unobstructed.

questions.stem: A 74-year-old man with Takotsubo cardiomyopathy develops profound hypotension and cardiogenic shock. Echocardiography reveals a dynamic left ventricular outflow tract (LVOT) gradient due to basal hyperkinesis. His systolic blood pressure is 75 mmHg. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: IV fluids increase preload, reducing LVOT obstruction. Dobutamine and other inotropes worsen the gradient and are contraindicated. Beta-blockers (e.g., metoprolol) can be used after fluid resuscitation if obstruction persists, but fluids are first-line. IABP and noradrenaline are not first-line.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman with Takotsubo cardiomyopathy is found to have a left ventricular thrombus on echocardiography performed 5 days after admission. She is haemodynamically stable and in sinus rhythm. What is the most appropriate treatment?
questions.explanation: Left ventricular thrombus in Takotsubo requires anticoagulation to prevent embolic events. Warfarin (vitamin K antagonist) is the standard first-line agent for LV thrombus in the UK. DOACs are not yet established for this indication. Aspirin and clopidogrel are antiplatelet agents and insufficient.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman with Takotsubo cardiomyopathy has ongoing heart failure symptoms (NYHA class II) 2 weeks after presentation. Echocardiography shows left ventricular ejection fraction of 35% with no LVOT obstruction. Her blood pressure is 120/80 mmHg and renal function is normal. What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacological therapy?
questions.explanation: For heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF), an ACE inhibitor is first-line to reduce mortality and morbidity. Beta-blockers are added later once stable. Diuretics are for congestion, not first-line chronic therapy. Digoxin and ivabradine are second-line.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman experiences a second episode of Takotsubo cardiomyopathy, 18 months after her first episode. Both episodes were triggered by emotional stress. She has fully recovered with normal cardiac function between episodes. What is the most appropriate long-term therapy to reduce recurrence risk?
questions.explanation: Beta-blockers are commonly recommended to blunt surges in catecholamines that trigger Takotsubo, potentially reducing recurrence. SSRIs may address underlying stress but are not first-line for recurrence prevention. ACE inhibitors, aspirin, and no therapy have no proven benefit for recurrence.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman presents with acute central chest pain after her husband's funeral. ECG shows ST-segment elevation in leads V2–V5. High-sensitivity troponin I is 450 ng/L (normal < 34). She is normotensive and saturations 97% on air. Bedside echocardiography reveals apical ballooning with mid-ventricular hypokinesis. Coronary angiography is not immediately available. What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Reassure the patient and discharge with outpatient echo follow-up
Start oral ramipril 2.5 mg once daily
Admit to coronary care unit for observation
Administer aspirin 300 mg orally and perform urgent coronary angiography
questions.explanation: Takotsubo cardiomyopathy is a diagnosis of exclusion. The patient presents with STEMI-like symptoms, and urgent coronary angiography is essential to rule out acute coronary syndrome (ACS) before attributing the presentation to Takotsubo. Although beta-blockers and ACE inhibitors are used in management, angiographic confirmation takes precedence. Discharge is inappropriate due to the risk of ACS.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman presents with chest tightness and mild dyspnoea following a recent bereavement. ECG shows deep T-wave inversion in the anterior leads. High-sensitivity troponin I is 120 ng/L (normal < 34). Blood pressure 125/80 mmHg, heart rate 90 bpm. Bedside echo shows left ventricular apical hypokinesis with preserved basal function. She is pain-free and haemodynamically stable. What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Start oral bisoprolol 2.5 mg once daily
Arrange cardiac MRI for tissue characterisation
Initiate low-dose ramipril 1.25 mg once daily
questions.explanation: Despite the classic apical ballooning on echo, coronary artery disease must be excluded with coronary angiography before diagnosing Takotsubo cardiomyopathy. The presence of elevated troponin and T-wave inversion raises the possibility of non-ST elevation ACS. Starting beta-blocker or ACE inhibitor without angiographic confirmation is premature, and MRI is a later step.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman presents with severe central chest pain and breathlessness. ECG shows ST-elevation in V2–V5. Systolic BP 85 mmHg, heart rate 110 bpm, oxygen saturations 88% on 15 L/min non-rebreather. Auscultation reveals bilateral crackles. Transthoracic echo shows apical ballooning with hyperdynamic basal segments. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Administer intravenous fluids (500 mL crystalloid stat)
questions.explanation: This patient presents with cardiogenic shock and suspected STEMI. Emergency coronary angiography is the priority to differentiate Takotsubo cardiomyopathy from acute myocardial infarction and to identify any obstructive coronary artery disease that may require revascularisation. Supportive measures (e.g. CPAP, furosemide) are important but should not delay definitive angiography. Fluids may worsen pulmonary oedema, and IABP is not first-line.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman with known Takotsubo cardiomyopathy (diagnosed 2 weeks ago) presents with acute-onset dyspnoea. Blood pressure 90/60 mmHg, heart rate 105 bpm. ECG shows sinus tachycardia and resolving T-wave inversion. Repeat echocardiography reveals a new left ventricular outflow tract (LVOT) gradient of 50 mmHg at rest with systolic anterior motion of the mitral valve. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Administer a cautious intravenous fluid bolus (e.g. 250 mL 0.9% sodium chloride)
Start an intravenous beta-blocker (e.g. metoprolol 5 mg)
questions.explanation: LVOT obstruction in Takotsubo cardiomyopathy is worsened by hypovolaemia and inotropes. The immediate step is to cautiously give IV fluids to increase preload and reduce the obstruction. Beta-blockers are first-line for LVOTO but may exacerbate hypotension in this unstable patient; fluids take precedence. Dobutamine would increase obstruction, IABP is not indicated, and MRI is not urgent.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman was diagnosed with Takotsubo cardiomyopathy 3 months ago. She was started on bisoprolol 2.5 mg once daily and has been asymptomatic. Repeat echocardiography shows normal left ventricular function with no residual wall motion abnormality. She asks what she can do to reduce her risk of recurrence. What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Switch bisoprolol to verapamil 120 mg once daily
Discuss stress reduction techniques and avoidance of triggers
questions.explanation: Recurrence of Takotsubo cardiomyopathy is strongly associated with emotional or physical triggers. The most appropriate immediate action is to counsel the patient on stress management and trigger avoidance. There is no evidence that ACE inhibitors or calcium channel blockers reduce recurrence, and repeat angiography is not indicated unless new symptoms develop. Cardiac rehabilitation may be considered but is not the first step.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old postmenopausal woman presents with acute central chest pain following an argument. ECG shows ST elevation in V2-V5 without reciprocal ST depression in the inferior leads. High-sensitivity troponin I is 150 ng/L (normal <34). Which finding most strongly supports a diagnosis of takotsubo cardiomyopathy rather than acute anterior STEMI?
options: Hypercontractility of basal segments on echocardiography
Absence of reciprocal ST depression on ECG
Mild troponin elevation relative to the extent of wall motion abnormality
questions.explanation: The absence of reciprocal ST depression in inferior leads is a well-recognised ECG clue differentiating takotsubo from anterior STEMI, where reciprocal changes are common. This can prevent inappropriate thrombolysis.

questions.stem: A 66-year-old woman with known takotsubo cardiomyopathy becomes acutely hypotensive (BP 80/50 mmHg) and develops a new loud systolic murmur at the left sternal edge. She is in sinus tachycardia. Which echocardiographic finding is most likely responsible?
options: Severe mitral regurgitation due to papillary muscle rupture
Moderate pericardial effusion with early tamponade
Dynamic left ventricular outflow tract obstruction with systolic anterior motion of the mitral valve
questions.explanation: Dynamic LVOT obstruction is a known complication due to basal hyperkinesis and apical hypokinesis, causing systolic anterior motion of the mitral valve and a systolic murmur. It requires specific management (fluids, beta-blockers, avoid inotropes).

questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman presents with acute chest pain after the death of her husband. ECG shows deep T wave inversions in the precordial leads and a QTc interval of 520 ms. She is haemodynamically stable. Which complication is she at highest risk of developing?
questions.explanation: A prolonged QTc interval (>500 ms) in takotsubo predisposes to torsade de pointes, a life-threatening arrhythmia that requires monitoring and electrolyte optimisation.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman with takotsubo cardiomyopathy is admitted with dyspnoea and LVEF 35% with apical akinesia on echocardiography. On day 3, she develops sudden aphasia and right-sided hemiparesis. Which finding on transoesophageal echocardiography is most likely to be identified?
options: Patent foramen ovale with right-to-left shunt
questions.explanation: Apical akinesia in takotsubo predisposes to left ventricular thrombus formation, which can embolise to the brain causing ischaemic stroke. Anticoagulation may be considered.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with takotsubo cardiomyopathy is stable on the ward but has persistent dyspnoea and LVEF 35% on echocardiography. Which additional echocardiographic finding is associated with higher in-hospital mortality and may prompt escalation of care?
questions.explanation: Right ventricular involvement in takotsubo indicates more extensive myocardial stunning and is independently associated with worse outcomes including cardiogenic shock and death, warranting closer monitoring.
